David could not refuse Achish without danger. If he promised assistance and then remained neutral or went over to the Israelites, he would behave with ingratitude and treachery. If he fought against Israel, he would sin greatly. It seemed impossible that he should get out of this difficulty with a clear conscience; but his evasive answer, intended to gain time, was not consistent with the character of an Israelite indeed. Troubles are terrors to those who disobey. In his distress, Saul inquired of the Lord. He did not seek in faith, but with a double, unstable mind. Saul had put the law in force against those who dealt with spirits. Many seem zealous against sin when they are hurt by it, and have no concern for the glory of God, nor any dislike of sin as sin. Many seem enemies to sin in others while they indulge it in themselves. Saul will drive out evil from his kingdom, yet harbors it in his heart through envy and malice. How foolish to consult those whom, according to God's law, he had tried to destroy!

When we go from the clear path of duty, everything draws us further aside and increases our confusion and temptation. Saul wanted the woman to bring someone back from the dead so that he could speak with them; this was expressly forbidden. All real or pretended witchcraft or conjuration is a malicious or ignorant attempt to gain knowledge or help from some creature when it cannot be had from the Lord in the path of duty. While Samuel was living, we never read of Saul going to advise with him in any difficulties; it would have been well for him if he had. But now that he was dead, he said, “Bring me up Samuel.” Many who despise and persecute God’s saints and ministers when they are alive would be glad to have them again when they are gone. The whole shows that it was no human fraud or trick. Though the woman could not cause Samuel's appearance, Saul's inquiry might be the occasion of it. The woman's surprise and terror proved that it was an unusual and unexpected appearance. Saul had despised Samuel’s solemn warnings in his lifetime, yet now that he hoped, as a defiance of God, to obtain some counsel and encouragement from him, might not God permit the soul of his departed prophet to appear to Saul to confirm his former sentence and denounce his doom? The expression, “You and your sons shall be with me,” means no more than that they shall be in the eternal world. There appears much solemnity in God’s permitting the soul of a departed prophet to come as a witness from heaven to confirm the word he had spoken on earth.

Those who expect any good counsel or comfort other than from God and in the way of his institutions will be as wretchedly disappointed as Saul. Though terrified even to despair, he was not humbled. He confessed not his sins, offered no sacrifices, and presented no supplications. He does not seem to have cared about his sons or his people, nor did he attempt any escape; but in sullen despair he rushed upon his doom. God sets up a few such beacons to warn people not to stifle convictions or despise his word. But while one repenting thought remains, let no sinner suppose himself in this case. Let him humble himself before God, determined to live and die beseeching his favor, and he will succeed.
